messages.content:
Which of the following statement/s is true? *
a. Split thickness skin graft has more dermal content than Full thickness skin
graft
b. Secondary contraction is greatest in Full thickness skin grafts making it ideal
for extensive deep partial to full thickness burns
c. Full thickness skin grafts exhibits the least secondary contraction making it ideal for areas of aesthetic concern (eg face)
d. All of the choices is/are correct
c. Full thickness skin grafts exhibits the least secondary contraction making it ideal for areas of aesthetic concern (eg face)
rationalize

Full thickness skin grafts include the entire thickness of the skin, including the epidermis and dermis. Since it has a greater amount of dermal tissue, it is less prone to secondary contraction, which is the gradual shrinking of the skin graft as it heals, resulting in tissue distortion. This characteristic makes full thickness skin grafts an ideal option for areas of aesthetic concern, such as the face, where minimal distortion is desired.

Which of the following statement/s is true? * rationalize the choices
a. Split thickness skin graft has more dermal content than Full thickness skin graft
b. Secondary contraction is greatest in Full thickness skin grafts making it ideal for extensive deep partial to full thickness burns
c. Full thickness skin grafts exhibits the least secondary contraction making it ideal for areas of aesthetic concern (eg face)
d. All of the choices is/are correct

The correct statement is c. Full thickness skin grafts exhibits the least secondary contraction making it ideal for areas of aesthetic concern (eg face).

a. Split thickness skin grafts include only a portion of the dermal layer, making it less dense than full thickness skin grafts. Therefore, split thickness skin grafts have less dermal content than full thickness skin grafts. This statement is incorrect.

b. Secondary contraction is greatest in full thickness skin grafts, which makes it an ideal alternative for deep partial to full thickness burns. This statement is incorrect as well.

c. Full thickness skin grafts include both the epidermis and dermis, which makes it a thicker and more structured graft than split thickness skin grafts. This characteristic is what makes full thickness skin grafts less prone to secondary contraction and thus, an optimal choice for areas requiring little skin distortion, such as the face.

d. Statement a and b are incorrect, which makes statement d incorrect. The correct statement is c.

To obtain a fine line scar on the face, skin sutures are
removed within how many days? *
a. 7
b. 2-4
c. 4-5
d. 5-6
explain

To obtain a fine line scar on the face, skin sutures are removed within 2-4 days after surgery. This quick removal of sutures minimizes any tension on the skin, which can lead to wider, more visible scars.

Removing sutures early also prevents the sutures from becoming embedded into the skin, which can cause further tissue damage or inflammation. By removing sutures after 2-4 days, the skin has had enough time to begin healing and holding its shape, allowing the incision to come together with minimal tension. This promotes cosmetic outcomes with a thin line scar.
what happens if its removed 4-5 days

If skin sutures are removed 4-5 days after surgery, it may lead to increased skin tension as the suture materials start to become more adherent to the skin. This can cause more damage to the surrounding tissues and may result in wider and more noticeable scars. Additionally, delayed suture removal can cause more stress on the wound, making it more prone to infection and dehiscence (splitting open of the wound).

Moreover, after 4-5 days, the skin may begin to create more scar tissue, making it more challenging to produce a fine line scar. Therefore, it is essential to remove skin sutures within 2-4 days for optimal healing and cosmetic outcomes.

The inciting event in Pierre Robin Sequence which may
eventually lead to the clinical presentation of respiratory
compromise: *
a. Mandibular hypoplasia
b. airway obstruction
c. cleft palate
d. glossoptosis
d. Glossoptosis
explain

Pierre Robin Sequence (PRS) is a congenital disorder that presents with a combination of features including micrognathia (mandibular hypoplasia), glossoptosis, and cleft palate. The hallmark feature of PRS is glossoptosis, which refers to the posterior displacement of the tongue towards the pharynx. It happens because of the altered positioning of the maxilla and mandible, which causes the base of the tongue to be pushed backward.

The tongue's backward displacement enlarges the posterior pharynx and inhibits the soft palate from elevating, which leads to an obstructed airway. The glossoptosis leads to a small jaw, which means that there is less space for the tongue to fit. This, in turn, causes resistance to incoming air, lowering oxygen levels in the body. Thus, respiratory compromise can occur, which is the primary concern with PRS.

Though cleft palate can be present in many cases of PRS, it is not the primary factor for respiratory compromise. On the other hand, mandibular hypoplasia, and airway obstruction are secondary features of PRS; they are the consequences of glossoptosis.
explain the other choices

Mandibular hypoplasia is a condition where the mandible is underdeveloped, leading to a receded or small chin. It is a feature of PRS and is caused by a deficiency in the prenatal growth rate of the jaw.

Airway obstruction can occur in PRS when the posterior displacement of the tongue towards the pharynx results in the obstruction of the upper airway. The decreased airflow in the respiratory system can cause hypoxia (low oxygen levels), which leads to respiratory distress.

Cleft palate is a congenital disorder that affects the development of the oral cavity. Infants born with cleft palate have an opening in the roof of their mouth that can interfere with feeding, speech development, and hearing. Cleft palate can be present in many cases of PRS, but it is not the primary factor leading to respiratory compromise, unlike glossoptosis.
can mandibular hypoplasia present with respiratory compromise

Yes, Mandibular hypoplasia (a underdeveloped or receded chin) can sometimes present with respiratory compromise, especially in severe cases. When the lower jaw (mandible) is short or underdeveloped, it can result in a narrow airway and cause obstruction to breathing, especially in the supine position (lying on the back). This narrowing of the airway leads to an increase in air resistance, which can result in snoring, hypopnea, gasping, and difficulty breathing during sleep. In some cases, mandibular hypoplasia can also cause obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) - a sleep disorder characterized by intermittent cessation of breathing. Therefore, mandibular hypoplasia and respiratory compromise have a close relationship, especially when it comes to breathing difficulties during sleep.

The inciting event in Pierre Robin Sequence which may
eventually lead to the clinical presentation of respiratory
compromise:

The inciting event in Pierre Robin Sequence (PRS) which may eventually lead to the clinical presentation of respiratory compromise is glossoptosis. Glossoptosis is the posterior displacement of the tongue towards the pharynx due to mandibular micrognathia (small jaw) and upper airway obstruction caused by the cleft palate. The glossoptosis enlarges the posterior pharynx and inhibits the soft palate from elevating, leading to an obstructed airway, making it difficult for the baby to breathe. This, in turn, can cause respiratory distress, often characterized by an increased work of breathing, cyanosis, and hypoxemia. Hence, glossoptosis is considered the primary cause of respiratory compromise in infants with Pierre Robin Sequence.
is mandibular hypoplasia part of prs

Yes, Mandibular hypoplasia is a component of Pierre Robin Sequence (PRS). PRS is a congenital condition that primarily includes the triad of micrognathia (small jaw), cleft palate, and glossoptosis (downward displacement or retraction of the tongue into the pharynx). Mandibular hypoplasia refers to an underdeveloped mandible (lower jaw) and is a significant component of micrognathia. Infants born with PRS often exhibit mandibular hypoplasia, which can contribute to airway obstruction and difficulties with breathing. Therefore, mandibular hypoplasia is an essential feature of PRS and can significantly impact the overall prognosis and management of the condition.
but does it cause respiratory compromise

Yes, mandibular hypoplasia can cause respiratory compromise in some cases. A small or receded chin can result in underdevelopment of the lower jaw, and the underdeveloped mandible can lead to narrowing of the pharyngeal airway. This narrowing of the airway can cause resistance to incoming air and lead to breathing difficulties, especially during sleep. In severe cases, a small jaw can contribute to upper airway obstruction, including obstructive sleep apnea (OSA), which is characterized by snoring, frequent pauses in breathing during sleep, and low blood oxygen levels. Therefore, mandibular hypoplasia can present with respiratory compromise, and it is essential to assess the degree of obstruction and consider early intervention in such cases.
inciting meaning

"Inciting" refers to something that initiates or has caused a particular event or situation to occur. It is used to describe the action or event that sets off a chain reaction, leading to a particular outcome or consequence. The inciting event is usually the first occurrence that starts the trajectory of a particular situation and is therefore critical in understanding the sequence of events that follow it. In medicine, the inciting event is often the underlying cause of a particular condition or disease. Understanding the inciting event is essential in determining the optimal approach to management, treatment, and prevention of the condition or disease.

d. glossoptosis
a. Mandibular hypoplasia

which is the inciting event in prs

The inciting event in Pierre Robin Sequence (PRS) is glossoptosis. Glossoptosis is the posterior displacement of the tongue towards the pharynx due to mandibular micrognathia (small jaw) and upper airway obstruction caused by the cleft palate. The tongue's posterior displacement towards the pharynx is the initial event that sets off a chain of events that lead to the clinical presentation of respiratory compromise in PRS. The glossoptosis enlarges the posterior pharynx and inhibits the soft palate from elevating, leading to an obstructed airway, making it difficult for the baby to breathe. Therefore, glossoptosis is considered the primary cause/inciting event of respiratory compromise in infants with Pierre Robin Sequence. Mandibular hypoplasia is a characteristic feature of PRS, and it contributes to the overall clinical presentation, but it is not considered the inciting event.

Which structure in the dermis is responsible for the the
strength and mechanical integrity of the wound repair?
a. elastin
b. glygocaminoglycans
c. fibronectin
d. collagen

d. collagen is responsible for the strength and mechanical integrity of the wound repair.

Collagen is a primary structural protein found in the extracellular matrix of the dermis, tendons, and bones. It provides strength and elasticity to tissues and is the essential component of the wound matrix. During the wound healing process, fibroblasts produce collagen and deposit it in the extracellular matrix, forming the bulk of the wound repair. Collagen fibers offer mechanical stability and support to the healing wound and help rebuild the damaged tissue's structural integrity. The deposition of collagen in the wound repair is a multi-step process that can take up to several weeks or months to complete. With time, the deposited collagen remodels and matures, leading to an organized and robust wound repair, providing strength to the healed tissue. Hence, collagen is the critical component responsible for the mechanical integrity and strength of the wound repair.
